rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions (the top 8 and bottom 13 of the original ranking); ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank and the published set is a bimodal artifact, not a representative distribution. New York does not currently appear in the published index at all and must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt — the published set contains only the original top 8 and bottom 13 with ranks 9-38 missing; see rank_note). Calibrated to methodology anchors and against the other five states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Ohio 38.1, Michigan 51.9 (superseded, corrected 53.8), Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9. Calibration caveat (from the underlying assessment): New York received a deep, adversarial, source-by-source evidence review across all 40 subdimensions, while published peers such as Connecticut, Minnesota and Massachusetts carry scores from the original index build with a lighter evidentiary touch and conspicuously round, uniform dimension values (published Connecticut scores exactly 4.0 on six of eight dimensions); New York's gap to those peers should therefore be read as evidence of closer examination, not proof of materially lower compassion, until the published peers receive equivalent-depth review. Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs: dimension mean 3.275, baseComposite 56.875, stdDev 0.4235 (consistencyMult 1.0), 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 (highest is SYS at 3.8) so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Function returns {"composite":56.9,"band":"Functional"} — matches the target exactly. Note: the underlying assessment prose states 'but 6 of 8 dimensions fall below 4.0' when in fact all 8 are below 4.0; this is a wording discrepancy in the source document with no effect on the result, since weaknessFactor clips to 0 under either count.
attribution_note: Scores New York's own state conduct. Federal interaction was explicitly excluded from scoring: the loss of Essential Plan coverage for 450,000 people on 2026-07-01 was triggered by federal law (H.R. 1, enacted July 2025); New York was scored only on its own response to that shock — specifically the reported absence of a state mitigation plan — and not on the federal action itself (EQU/ACT). By contrast, the June 2026 immigration package — barring 287(g) agreements, phasing out local ICE detention contracts by late 2026, creating a private right of action against federal officials, and establishing an Office of Immigrant Trust with enforcement power over non-compliant local governments — is New York's own discretionary confrontation with federal enforcement priorities and is scored positively (SYS/S4, INT/I1). State-versus-city attribution was applied carefully on homelessness: much of the sharpest data is New York City-specific, and the assessment scored the state's own constitutional right-to-shelter obligation and its $4.3 billion asylum-seeker commitment as state conduct while avoiding scoring municipal execution as state conduct. The 2021 nursing-home death undercount, the Robert Brooks and Messiah Nantwi killings by state corrections staff, the HALT Act suspension and subsequent court ruling, and the CDPAP home-care consolidation are all entirely state-level actions with no federal component and are fully scored (ACC, BND).
key_evidence: New York helps at enormous scale but cannot hold itself accountable when it causes harm: it scores 65 on Equity and 70 on Systemic Thinking but only 40 on Accountability, and two men — Robert Brooks and Messiah Nantwi — were beaten to death by state prison staff within three months of each other (December 2024 and March 2025).

The state's own numbers show its prison discipline system does not work: of roughly 1,200 excessive-force complaints in New York prisons in 2025, the corrections department substantiated fewer than 50 (about 4.2%), and when staff were fired for cause, independent arbitrators returned them to their jobs in every single case.

New York suspended its own HALT solitary-confinement law during a February 2025 guard strike; a judge ruled in July 2025 that this violated the separation of powers, after an August 2024 Inspector General report had already found 40% of people held in solitary longer than the law allowed.

The people who deliver New York's compassion are depleted: state employees worked 22.2 million overtime hours in 2022 at a cost of $1.36 billion, and chronic understaffing contributed to an illegal corrections officer strike that required the Governor to declare a disaster emergency and activate the National Guard.

Real, measurable wins exist alongside the failures: overdose deaths fell from 6,688 (2023) to about 4,567 (2024), a 32% drop; New York served 396 million free school meals to 2.7 million students in 2025-26; and the 2021 Excluded Workers Fund paid $2.1 billion to over 130,000 workers excluded from federal aid, a politically costly act other states later copied.
